text_blocks.text: The historical measure of a steam
engine's energy efficiency was its "duty". The
concept of duty was first introduced by Watt in
order to illustrate how much more efficient his
engines were over the earlier Newcomen designs.
Duty is the number of foot-pounds of work
delivered by burning one bushel (94 pounds) of
coal. The best examples of Newcomen designs had
a duty of about 7 million, but most were closer
to 5 million. Watt's original low-pressure designs
were able to deliver duty as high as 25 million,
but averaged about 17. This was a three-fold
improvement over the average Newcomen design.
Early Watt engines equipped with high-pressure
